question_text: Starting from inventing the 'basic structure' doctrine, the judiciary has played a highly proactive role in ensuring that India develops into a thriving democracy. In light of the statement, evaluate the role played by judicial activism in achieving the ideals of democracy. (200 words)

points.text: Basic structure doctrine (Kesavananda Bharati, 1973) – judiciary as guardian of constitutional morality; judicial activism expanded democratic ideals

Contributions: expanded fundamental rights (Right to life includes livelihood, clean environment, privacy), checked executive excesses (2G, coal scam), protected secularism (Hindutva verdict), enforced accountability (CBI monitoring, Lokpal oversight)
Examples: S.R. Bommai case (safeguarded federalism against misuse of Article 356), Vishaka guidelines (sexual harassment at workplace), NALSA (transgender rights), decriminalization of Section 377
Criticisms: judicial overreach, encroachment into executive/legislative domain (e.g., ban on firecrackers, odd-even scheme), lack of accountability, delay in justice, backlog
Judicial activism strengthened democracy but needs balance with separation of powers; public interest litigation (PIL) remains a vital tool for marginalized

question_text: Though the federal principle is dominant in our Constitution and that principle is one of its basic features, but it is equally true that federalism under the Indian Constitution leans in favour of a strong Centre, a feature that militates against the concept of strong federalism. Discuss. (200 words)

points.text: Indian Constitution is federal with unitary bias; basic structure includes federalism, but Centre dominant (Article 1: Union of States)

Unitary features: single citizenship, strong Centre (Union List, Concurrent List with override), emergency provisions (Articles 352, 356, 360), appointment of Governors by President, All India Services, integrated judiciary, Parliament's power to legislate on state subjects in certain conditions
Federal features: written Constitution, distribution of powers (three lists), independent judiciary, bicameral legislature (Rajya Sabha representing states), supremacy of Constitution

Contradiction: strong Centre was deliberate (partition, unity, integrity); but this militates against strong federalism (states' autonomy curtailed, fiscal dependence, Article 356 misuse historically, GST Council domination by Centre)
Indian federalism is 'cooperative' and 'asymmetric' (special provisions for some states); need for more fiscal federalism and state autonomy within Union framework

question_text: The 'Powers, Privileges and Immunities of Parliament and its Members' as envisaged in Article 105 of the Constitution leave room for a large number of un-codified and un-enumerated privileges to continue. Assess the reasons for the absence of legal codification of the 'parliamentary privileges'. How can this problem be addressed? (200 words)

points.text: Article 105 grants Parliament and members privileges similar to House of Commons (UK) until codified by law; many remain uncodified, leading to ambiguity

Reasons for absence of codification: parliamentary sovereignty (legislature reluctant to regulate itself), fear of judicial review, political consensus elusive, privileges seen as essential for independence (freedom of speech, arrest immunity, right to exclude strangers)

Problems due to non-codification: conflict with fundamental rights (e.g., breach of privilege notices for criticism of MPs – violation of Article 19), arbitrary actions, lack of clarity, recent cases (arrest of journalists for reporting, suspension of MPs for showing placards)

How to address: enact Parliamentary Privileges Act codifying privileges (recommended by National Commission to Review Working of Constitution, 2002); subject privileges to fundamental rights (Article 19(2) already does but need clarity); define contempt and breach of privilege precisely; allow judicial review of parliamentary actions affecting rights
Codification necessary to balance parliamentary independence with citizens' rights and rule of law; India should follow Australia, Canada which have codified

question_text: What do you understand by the concept "freedom of speech and expression"? Does it cover hate speech also? Why do the films in India stand on a slightly different plane from other forms of expression? Discuss. (200 words)

points.text: Freedom of speech and expression (Article 19(1)(a)) – right to express opinions, ideas, and information without state interference, subject to reasonable restrictions under Article 19(2)

Hate speech: not covered; restrictions under 19(2) include public order, decency, incitement to offence; hate speech (speech attacking religious/racial groups) can be restricted; Supreme Court in Pravasi Bhalai Sangathan (2014) upheld restrictions; but no specific hate speech law, only IPC Sections 153A, 295A, 505

Films different plane: pre-censorship (Cinematograph Act, 1952) – unlike print media (only post-publication action); reasons: films have greater impact (audio-visual, mass reach, emotional appeal), need to protect public order and morality, prevent communal tension; also self-regulation via CBFC (U/A, A certificates)

Criticism: prior restraint violates free speech (K.A. Abbas case 1971 upheld but with safeguards); demand for self-regulation; OTT platforms now under IT rules 2021 (no pre-censorship but code of ethics)
Freedom of speech fundamental but not absolute; hate speech not protected; films subject to pre-censorship due to unique impact, but balance needed to avoid chilling effect

question_text: Instances of President's delay in commuting death sentences has come under public debate as denial of justice. Should there be a time limit specified for the President to accept/reject such petitions? Analyse. (200 words)

points.text: President's power to grant pardon under Article 72 (Governor under 161); delays in deciding mercy petitions cause prolonged uncertainty and mental torture to convicts, raising human rights concerns

Current position: no time limit; Supreme Court in Shatrughan Chauhan (2014) held that inordinate delay (over 7-8 years) can commute death sentence to life imprisonment; but President can take years (e.g., Afzal Guru's petition pending 8 years before execution)

Argument for time limit: ensures right to speedy justice (Article 21), reduces mental agony, prevents arbitrary delays, brings certainty; international obligations (ICCPR) suggest prompt review; recommendation by Law Commission (262nd report on death penalty)

Counter-arguments: President needs time to consult, examine voluminous records; constitutional office cannot be bound by strict time limit (executive discretion); possible misuse to file multiple petitions; security concerns in terror cases
Reasonable time limit (e.g., 6 months) can be prescribed without compromising fairness; SC's 'delay as ground for commutation' partly addresses issue, but codified timeline would enhance justice

question_text: The size of the cabinet should be as big as governmental work justifies and as big as the Prime Minister can manage as a team. How far is the efficacy of a government then inversely related to the size of the cabinet? Discuss. (200 words)

points.text: Cabinet size in India governed by Constitution (91st Amendment Act 2003 – limits to 15% of Lok Sabha strength; currently max 81 ministers), but actual size varies (around 70-80 in Union)

Arguments for inverse relation: large cabinet leads to coordination problems, diluted accountability, increased expenditure, factionalism, reduced efficiency (Parkinson's law); smaller cabinet facilitates quicker decisions, cohesive team, collective responsibility

Counter-arguments: diversity requires representation of regions, castes, allies; large coalition governments need accommodation; size not the only factor – quality, leadership, delegation matter; US cabinet small but slower due to checks; India's large cabinet still functions (e.g., UPA-II)

Empirical evidence: studies show no strict inverse correlation; efficacy depends on internal coordination, PM's leadership, ministerial competence, and bureaucratic support. India's cabinet size remained near ceiling but governance efficacy varied across PMs

Inverse relationship not absolute; beyond a threshold, large cabinet reduces efficiency, but optimal size depends on context; constitutional ceiling adequate, but implementation should emphasize quality over quantity

question_text: Though 100 percent FDI is already allowed in non-news media like a trade publication and general entertainment channel, the Government is mulling over the proposal for increased FDI in news media for quite some time. What difference would an increase in FDI make? Critically evaluate the pros and cons. (200 words)

points.text: Current FDI limit in news media: 26% (print) and 49% (digital news) under government route; proposal to raise limits debated for years

Pros of increased FDI: capital infusion for struggling media houses, technology upgradation, professional management, global best practices, competition and quality improvement, employment generation, reduced political/business influence (if diversified ownership)

Cons: threat to editorial independence (foreign influence), risk of monopolization, national security concerns (news as strategic sector), loss of Indian voice in media, possible manipulation of public opinion, job losses if foreign entities restructure
Difference: increased FDI would bring more foreign control, potentially reduce plurality; safeguards needed like press freedom, editorial autonomy, cross-media ownership limits, antitrust oversight
Balanced approach: moderate increase (49%) with strong safeguards, retaining Indian majority control; news media as fourth estate needs protection from both state and corporate capture

question_text: The setting up of a Rail Tariff Authority to regulate fares will subject the cash strapped Indian Railways to demand subsidy for obligation to operate non-profitable routes and services. Taking into account the experience in the power sector, discuss if the proposed reform is expected to benefit the consumers, the Indian Railways or the private container operators. (200 words)

points.text: Rail Tariff Authority (RTA) proposed to determine passenger and freight fares independently, like electricity regulators (CERC/SERCs), to depoliticize pricing

Power sector experience: independent regulators improved tariff rationalization but led to increase in retail tariffs; cross-subsidy persists; state electricity boards' losses shifted to consumers; regulatory capture and political interference still exist
Benefits for consumers: transparent, predictable fare revisions; may avoid sudden hikes; but fares likely to increase to cover costs – politically sensitive

For Indian Railways: RTA may force rationalization of cross-subsidy (freight subsidizes passenger); Railways may need explicit budget subsidy for non-profitable routes (social obligation). Could improve financial health if allowed to charge economic fares on profitable routes

For private container operators: likely benefit from more stable, non-discriminatory freight tariffs; level playing field with Railways (currently Railways uses freight to cross-subsidize passengers, hurting private logistics)
RTA beneficial if coupled with explicit government subsidy for social obligations; success depends on autonomy, expertise, and political will to accept tariff hikes

question_text: National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) in India can be most effective when its tasks are adequately supported by other mechanisms that ensure the accountability of a government. In light of the above observation assess the role of NHRC as an effective complement to the judiciary and other institutions in promoting and protecting human rights standards. (200 words)

points.text: NHRC (established 1993 under Protection of Human Rights Act) – statutory body, not constitutional; complements judiciary by handling human rights violations through inquiry and recommendations

Role as complement to judiciary: reduces burden on courts (NHRC can investigate and recommend compensation), focuses on systemic issues (custodial deaths, police brutality, prison conditions), provides speedy relief (though not binding, its recommendations have moral authority)

Limitations: NHRC lacks power to punish, cannot enforce its recommendations; dependent on government compliance; underfunded, understaffed; many recommendations ignored (e.g., police reforms, compensation for victims)

Need for other mechanisms: State Human Rights Commissions, National Commission for Women/SC/ST, CBI, Lokpal, RTI, and judiciary; coordination essential; NHRC's effectiveness hinges on government accountability (e.g., compliance reporting, budgetary support)

NHRC effective as catalyst and bridge, but needs enhanced powers (tribunal status) and political will for implementation; collective institutional ecosystem required for robust human rights protection

question_text: The penetration of Self Help Groups (SHGs) in rural areas in promoting participation in development programmes is facing socio-cultural hurdles. Examine. (200 words)

points.text: SHGs – important vehicles for women's empowerment, financial inclusion, and community participation (e.g., NRLM, SGSY). However, penetration faces socio-cultural barriers

Socio-cultural hurdles: patriarchy – women's mobility restricted, decision-making controlled by men; caste hierarchies – lower caste women excluded from upper caste SHGs; religious norms – purdah, conservative attitudes towards women working outside; illiteracy – fear of handling finances, low awareness
Other hurdles: lack of family support, time poverty (unpaid domestic work), resistance from local power structures (moneylenders, sarpanch), internal conflicts within groups, elite capture
Impact on participation: women reluctant to attend meetings, speak up, take leadership roles; programme benefits diverted to men; low retention and savings; difficulty in forming heterogeneous groups

Need for gender sensitization, male engagement, community mobilization, literacy programmes, and role models to overcome socio-cultural barriers; SHG success depends on culturally sensitive implementation

question_text: Do government's schemes for up-lifting vulnerable and backward communities by protecting required social resources for them, lead to their exclusion in establishing businesses in urban economies? (200 words)

points.text: Government schemes for vulnerable communities (SC/ST/OBC) include reservations, subsidies, land rights, education scholarships, and economic support (Mudra, Stand-Up India)

Potential for exclusion: protective discrimination (reservations in public sector) may not prepare for private urban economy; skill gaps; stigma of 'reserved category' affecting confidence; dependency on government jobs rather than entrepreneurship; geographical concentration in rural areas limits urban exposure

Counter-argument: schemes like Stand-Up India (loans for SC/ST/women entrepreneurs), PMEGP, Venture Capital Funds for SCs aim to promote urban businesses; affirmative action in public procurement; coaching for competitive exams; these reduce exclusion rather than cause it

Reasons for exclusion not due to schemes but due to: discrimination in private sector, lack of networks, urban housing issues, digital divide, and historical disadvantages; schemes partially address these

Schemes do not inherently cause exclusion; poorly designed implementation may trap communities in traditional roles; need for skill development, mentorship, and private sector linkage to enable urban business success

question_text: An athlete participates in Olympics for personal triumph and nation's glory; victors are showered with cash incentives by various agencies, on their return. Discuss the merit of state sponsored talent hunt and its cultivation as against the rationale of a reward mechanism as encouragement. (200 words)

points.text: India's Olympic performance historically low; two approaches: state-sponsored talent identification and long-term cultivation vs. ex-post reward mechanism

State-sponsored talent hunt (TOPS, Khelo India, SAI academies): merit – early identification, systematic coaching, infrastructure, nutrition, sports science; long-term pipeline; reduces financial burden on athletes; examples: Abhinav Bindra, Neeraj Chopra benefited from government support

Reward mechanism (cash incentives, jobs, land): merit – immediate motivation, recognition, attracts talent to sports; encourages medal winners; but post-facto, does not create ecosystem; may promote doping shortcuts; insufficient for grassroots

Limitations of state cultivation: bureaucratic delays, corruption, inadequate infrastructure in rural areas, lack of quality coaches, political interference; reward system alone cannot produce champions

Both needed: state-sponsored cultivation for grassroots to podium, and rewards for excellence; sustainable success requires integrated approach (Khelo India, TOPS, corporate sponsorships, and incentives)

question_text: Should the premier institutes like IITs/IIMs be allowed to retain premier status, allowed more academic independence in designing courses and also decide mode/criteria of selection of students. Discuss in light of the growing challenges. (200 words)

points.text: IITs/IIMs are institutes of national importance (through Acts of Parliament); debate on autonomy vs. accountability in light of global competition and social equity

Arguments for autonomy: promotes innovation, flexibility in curriculum, attracts global faculty, faster adaptation to industry needs (e.g., AI, data science courses), reduces bureaucratic delays; international ranking improvement; freedom to decide selection criteria (e.g., JEE pattern changes, supernumerary quotas)

Challenges: autonomy may lead to commercialization, high fees (exclusion of poor), dilution of reservation policies (quota implementation requires government oversight), quality control, accountability to Parliament

Balanced approach: IITs already have substantial academic autonomy under Institute of Eminence status; selection criteria (JEE Advanced) managed jointly; need to maintain social mandate (EWS, OBC, SC/ST reservations) while allowing flexibility in curriculum and research

Premier status should be retained but with accountability; increased academic independence desirable, but selection criteria should ensure equity and national integration; a middle path of 'regulated autonomy' is ideal

question_text: Has the Cadre based Civil Services Organization been the cause of slow change in India? Critically examine. (200 words)

points.text: Indian civil services (IAS, IPS, IFS etc.) are cadre-based (allocated to states/central services); designed for stability, neutrality, and all-India character

Arguments for slow change: cadre system leads to lack of specialization (generalist vs. specialist), frequent transfers (political interference), reluctance to take decisions (risk aversion), seniority-based promotions (not merit), parochial loyalties (state cadre bias), resistance to reforms (NPM, lateral entry)

Counter-arguments: cadre system ensures national integration, familiarity with local language/culture, continuity, and prevents elitism; slow change due to other factors (political will, red-tapism, colonial legacy) not solely cadre; examples of fast reforms in some states (Gujarat, Tamil Nadu) under same cadre system

Recent reforms: lateral entry (deputy secretary level), performance-linked incentives, fixed tenure, compulsory domain training, e-governance, mission-mode projects (AYUSHMAN Bharat, Swachh Bharat) showing change possible within cadre system
Cadre system not sole cause of slow change; it has strengths and weaknesses; need for structural reforms (specialization, fixed tenure, accountability) within the cadre framework to accelerate change

question_text: Two parallel run schemes of the Government, viz. the Aadhaar Card and NPR, one as voluntary and the other as compulsory, have led to debates at national levels and also litigations. On merits, discuss whether or not both schemes need run concurrently. Analyse the potential of the schemes to achieve developmental benefits and equitable growth. (200 words)

points.text: Aadhaar (UIDAI) – voluntary biometric ID (now made mandatory for many schemes after Supreme Court partial upholding); NPR (National Population Register) – compulsory demographic and biometric register under Citizenship Act

Differences: Aadhaar for resident identification (any resident), NPR for usual resident (citizen-centric, basis for NRC); Aadhaar voluntary vs NPR compulsory; data sharing limited by SC (Aadhaar not to be shared for surveillance)

Need for concurrent running: duplication of effort, cost (both collect similar data), confusion, privacy risks, litigation (Aadhaar case 2018) – SC allowed but limited use; NPR linked to Citizenship Amendment Act (CAA) and NRC, creating anxiety among minorities, especially in border states

Developmental benefits: Aadhaar enabled DBT (₹2 lakh crore savings), financial inclusion (Jan Dhan-Aadhaar-Mobile trinity), targeted subsidies (LPG, ration); NPR can help in national security, planning, and identifying undocumented migrants

Concurrent running not desirable due to overlap and legal challenges; ideally merge into single secure resident database with clear privacy safeguards; Aadhaar sufficient for development; NPR should be used only for specific citizenship/security purposes with transparency

question_text: With respect to the South China sea, maritime territorial disputes and rising tension affirm the need for safeguarding maritime security to ensure freedom of navigation and over flight throughout the region. In this context, discuss the bilateral issues between India and China. (200 words)

points.text: South China Sea (SCS) disputes involving China, Vietnam, Philippines, Malaysia, Brunei; China claims 90% based on nine-dash line; India has stakes due to trade (55% of sea trade passes through SCS) and strategic interests

Bilateral issues between India and China: (1) Border dispute (LAC) – Galwan clash (2020), Doklam standoff (2017); (2) India's energy exploration in SCS (Vietnam blocks) – China objects; (3) China's Belt and Road Initiative (CPEC through PoK) – violates India's sovereignty; (4) India's naval exercises (Malabar with US, Japan, Australia) – China views as containment; (5) China blocking India's NSG entry, UNSC permanent membership

India's stance on SCS: supports freedom of navigation, peaceful resolution under UNCLOS, opposes militarization; India conducts naval exercises, surveillance, and builds partnerships (Indonesia, Vietnam, Philippines) to protect sea lanes of communication (SLOC)

India-China rivalry extends to SCS; India must balance economic ties (trade) with strategic hedging, strengthen maritime capabilities (Andaman & Nicobar Command), and engage in multilateral forums (ASEAN, IORA) to safeguard interests

question_text: The aim of Information Technology Agreements (ITAs) is to lower all taxes and tariffs on information technology products by signatories to zero. What impact would such agreements have on India's interests? (200 words)

points.text: WTO's Information Technology Agreement (ITA) – plurilateral agreement eliminating tariffs on IT products; India joined ITA-1 (1997) but resisted ITA-2 (expansion, 2015) due to concerns

Positive impact: cheaper imports of IT hardware (computers, smartphones, components) benefit consumers and IT services industry (infosys, TCS) – lower costs for data centers, telecom; promotes digital inclusion, exports of IT-enabled services (zero tariff on their inputs)

Negative impact: domestic manufacturing of electronics (mobile phones, PCBs, LED panels) under Make in India suffers – cannot compete with duty-free imports from China, Taiwan, South Korea; loss of tariff revenue; no protection for nascent industries; job losses in electronics assembly

India's strategy: opted out of ITA-2 expansion; maintains tariffs on 200+ items (e.g., solar cells, medical devices) to support domestic manufacturing; uses Phased Manufacturing Program (PMP) for mobiles, PLI scheme to boost local production

ITA beneficial for services-oriented India but detrimental for manufacturing; India's cautious approach (not joining expansion) balances interests; need for calibrated liberalization with support for domestic industry

question_text: Some of the International funding agencies have special terms for economic participation stipulating a substantial component of the aid to be used for sourcing equipment from the leading countries. Discuss on merits of such terms and if, there exists a strong case not to accept such conditions in the Indian context. (200 words)

points.text: Tied aid – donor country requires recipient to spend aid on goods/services from donor; practice by export credit agencies, bilateral development agencies, and some MDBs (though reduced)

Merits for donor: promotes domestic industry, ensures quality (supposedly), creates jobs at home, maintains strategic influence; for recipient: may receive aid that otherwise not available, sometimes faster disbursal

Demerits for India: higher costs (donor goods often expensive), reduces competition, stifles local industry, technology lock-in (incompatible with Indian systems), delays due to customs/shipping, less value for money (e.g., tied aid up to 30% more expensive)

India's case: now a lower-middle-income country, prefers untied aid; self-reliance (Atmanirbhar Bharat) argues against tied aid; example: rejection of tied aid for solar projects (prefer domestic manufacturers); India capable of sourcing competitively from global market

Strong case to reject tied aid unless no alternatives or strategic necessity; India should push for untied aid, joint ventures, and technology transfer; OECD recommendation on untied aid for LDCs should be extended to India given its development stage

question_text: India has recently signed to become founding member of New Development Bank (NDB) and also the Asian Infrastructure Investment Bank (AIIB). How will the role of the two Banks be different? Discuss the strategic significance of these two Banks for India. (200 words)

points.text: NDB (BRICS Bank) – established 2015; AIIB (China-led) – operational 2016; India founding member of both (largest shareholder in NDB after BRICS countries, second largest in AIIB after China)

Differences in role: NDB focuses on infrastructure and sustainable development in BRICS and emerging economies (equal voting rights); AIIB focuses on Asia's infrastructure (China has 26% voting share). NDB has smaller portfolio, more democratic; AIIB larger, more China-influenced. Both complement World Bank/ADB

Strategic significance for India: (1) alternative to Western-dominated Bretton Woods institutions; (2) access to low-cost long-term loans (less conditionalities than IMF/WB); (3) infrastructure funding for India's needs (railways, ports, energy); (4) enhances India's global economic diplomacy; (5) NDB's headquarters in Shanghai but regional office in India (likely); (6) counters China's dominance in AIIB by being major shareholder
Risks: China's influence in AIIB may advance Belt and Road; India must ensure projects align with national interests, environmental standards, and avoid debt traps
NDB and AIIB strategically important for India's infrastructure financing needs and multipolar economic order; India should actively engage to shape their policies

question_text: WTO is an important international institution where decisions taken affect countries in a profound manner. What is the mandate of WTO and how binding are their decisions? Critically analyse India's stand on the latest round of talks on Food security. (200 words)

points.text: WTO – successor to GATT (1995); mandate: trade liberalization, dispute settlement, trade policy review, development issues (Doha Round)

Binding nature: WTO decisions (ministerial declarations) not automatically binding; but dispute settlement rulings (by panels and Appellate Body) are binding on members (loser must comply or face retaliation). Consensus-based decision making (except for amendments, but rarely used). Trade Policy Review reports non-binding

India's stand on Food security (Bali 2013, Nairobi 2015, ongoing): India seeks permanent solution for public stockholding for food security without being penalized under WTO's subsidy limits (de minimis 10% of production value). India argues that food procurement at MSP for PDS is not trade-distorting. India has used 'peace clause' (interim mechanism) to protect its programmes, but permanent solution still elusive

Criticism: India accused of being inflexible, blocking Doha Round; but India's stand justified given livelihood concerns and food security for poor; developed countries reluctant to give permanent solution due to pressure from agri-export lobbies

India's stand on food security is legitimate; WTO must accommodate developmental needs; India should continue pushing for permanent solution while reforming domestic subsidies to be more targeted and efficient
